Title: Great intro to informed search
explanation: The explanation of how informed search differs from uninformed search was crystal clear. Loved how the lecture broke down why heuristics help reduce the search space significantly.

Title: Uniform-cost search explained well
explanation: Finally understood UCS after this lecture. The priority queue explanation and the equivalence with BFS when all costs are equal was a great insight.

Title: Heuristic function concept clicked
explanation: The admissibility condition h(n) <= h*(n) was explained really intuitively. The idea that heuristic cost should never overestimate the true cost finally makes sense to me.

Title: Best-first search was confusing at first
explanation: I struggled with the greedy best-first search initially but the step-by-step algorithm walkthrough using OPEN and CLOSED lists helped a lot. Could use more examples though.

Title: A* is brilliant
explanation: A* combining UCS and greedy best-first search using f(n)=g(n)+h(n) is genuinely elegant. The fitness number concept tied everything together perfectly.

Title: Good lecture on UCS complexity
explanation: The time and space complexity analysis of uniform-cost search using O(b^(1+C*/ε)) was thorough. Appreciated the formal treatment.

Title: A* optimality proof needs more depth
explanation: The lecture mentioned A* is optimal but didn't fully prove why. Would have liked a deeper dive into consistency vs admissibility of heuristics.

Title: Loved the Romania map example
explanation: Using the Romania city map to implement A* in C++ was a fantastic real-world example. Seeing Arad to Bucharest solved step by step was very satisfying.

Title: UCS disadvantage is important
explanation: The note that UCS can get stuck in infinite loops because it ignores step count is critical and often overlooked. Glad it was highlighted.

Title: Best-first not optimal reminder
explanation: Good emphasis that greedy best-first search is NOT optimal and can behave like unguided DFS in worst case. Students often forget this distinction.

Title: Pure heuristic search overview
explanation: The description of pure heuristic search maintaining OPEN and CLOSED lists and expanding nodes with lowest h(n) was a solid foundation before diving into A*.

Title: Towers of Hanoi exercise was fun
explanation: The 2-disc Tower of Hanoi exercise with 9 states represented as a:(b c) notation was creative. Applying best-first and A* to it was challenging but rewarding.

Title: Completeness of UCS covered well
explanation: The lecture correctly states UCS is complete as long as a solution exists. Important property that distinguishes it from greedy approaches.

Title: BFS vs UCS comparison needed
explanation: Would have liked a side-by-side comparison of BFS and UCS to reinforce when they are equivalent and when they differ.

Title: Heuristic search more efficient
explanation: The point that informed search is more efficient for large search spaces because it uses domain knowledge to guide exploration is key. Well explained.

Title: OPEN and CLOSED list mechanics
explanation: The mechanics of maintaining OPEN and CLOSED lists in best-first search were explained clearly. The 7-step algorithm was easy to follow.

Title: A* exercise with multiple goals
explanation: The exercise with initial state S and two goal states O1 and O2 using the Manhattan-like heuristic H(S)=|xo-xs|+|yo-ys| was intellectually stimulating.

Title: Sorting by f value in A*
explanation: The sort step in A* where paths are ordered by f value ensures we always expand the most promising path first. The C++ sort function implementation made this concrete.

Title: Successor generation logic
explanation: The Successeurs function in C++ iterating over the adjacency matrix to find valid next nodes was a clean way to generate children in graph search.

Title: Path representation in A*
explanation: Storing the full path as an array inside the Node struct is an interesting implementation choice. Makes it easy to reconstruct the solution at the end.

Title: Greedy search time complexity
explanation: O(b^m) worst case for greedy best-first search is the same as DFS which confirms why it is not reliable for optimal solutions.

Title: Informed vs uninformed summary
explanation: The opening contrast between uninformed algorithms that explore blindly and informed ones that use heuristic knowledge was a great motivating framing for the chapter.

Title: h(n) always positive detail
explanation: Small but important: the lecture noted the heuristic function value is always positive. Easy to miss but matters for algorithm correctness.

Title: A* handles revisited nodes
explanation: The optimization condition in A* where we replace a path to a node only if the new g value is lower is what prevents suboptimal revisiting. Well explained.

Title: Greedy incomplete even on finite graphs
explanation: The fact that greedy best-first search is incomplete even on finite state spaces was surprising and well illustrated in the lecture.

Title: UCS is Dijkstra connection
explanation: UCS is essentially Dijkstra's algorithm. Would have been nice if the lecture made this connection explicitly for students with algorithms background.

Title: A* memory usage concern
explanation: A* can consume a lot of memory storing all paths. The lecture touched on this indirectly through the PATH struct with MAX_PATH limit of 100.

Title: Step by step trace was valuable
explanation: Walking through the A* memory state with paths like (B S) 5 7 and (A S) 3 8 and explaining why some paths are eliminated was very illuminating.

Title: DFS and BFS revisited in context
explanation: Good to revisit DFS and BFS as special cases within the informed search framework before moving to more powerful algorithms.

Title: Multiple goals in search problems
explanation: The exercise with two goal states O1 and O2 and different heuristic assignments per node group was a creative way to test understanding of A*.

Title: Fitness number naming convention
explanation: Calling f(n)=g(n)+h(n) the fitness number is a memorable label. It captures the idea that we score each node by total estimated path cost.

Title: Implementation details matter
explanation: The C++ implementation showed that real A* needs careful handling of edge cases like duplicate paths and comparison logic. Theory alone is not enough.

Title: Best-first vs A* key difference
explanation: Greedy best-first uses only h(n) while A* uses g(n)+h(n). This single difference makes A* optimal and greedy not. A profound distinction explained simply.

Title: Adjacency matrix for graph search
explanation: Using a 2D array A[8][8] to represent the city graph in C++ was straightforward and easy to understand for students familiar with matrices.

Title: UCS optimal property confirmed
explanation: UCS being always optimal because it selects minimum path cost at every step aligns with the definition of an optimal search algorithm.

Title: Hanoi state space was clever
explanation: Representing Tower of Hanoi states as peg positions of largest and smallest disks elegantly reduces the problem to a 9-node graph. Very clever problem design.

Title: Epsilon in UCS complexity
explanation: The use of epsilon as the minimum edge cost in the UCS complexity formula O(b^(1+C*/ε)) accounts for infinitely small steps. Important theoretical detail.

Title: A* terminates correctly
explanation: A* terminates when the goal node is at the top of the sorted memory. The condition check at the start of the recursive call is clean and correct.

Title: Graph vs tree search distinction
explanation: Would have appreciated a clearer distinction between graph search and tree search versions of these algorithms and how the CLOSED list handles the difference.

Title: Heuristic design is an art
explanation: The lecture touched on admissibility but designing good heuristics is genuinely difficult. More examples of admissible vs inadmissible heuristics would be helpful.

Title: Code walkthrough was helpful
explanation: Going through the C++ code line by line including the find function and the Adds function gave a practical sense of how abstract algorithms become real programs.

Title: Alphabetical ordering tiebreaker
explanation: The exercise instruction to use alphabetical order when nodes have equal priority is a practical detail that prevents ambiguity in manual traces.

Title: Overall excellent chapter
explanation: This chapter on informed search was one of the strongest in the course. The progression from UCS to heuristics to best-first to A* was logical and well-paced.

Title: BFS guarantees shortest path
explanation: Breadth-first search always finds the shortest path in terms of number of steps because it explores all nodes level by level before going deeper. This guarantee is its biggest strength.

Title: BFS memory is the real problem
explanation: BFS stores every node at the current frontier in memory. In a graph with branching factor 10 and depth 6 that is already a million nodes. The exponential memory growth is brutal.

Title: DFS goes deep immediately
explanation: Depth-first search dives straight down one branch before backtracking. It uses very little memory since it only stores nodes along the current path which is O(b*m) at most.

Title: DFS not complete on infinite graphs
explanation: If the search space has infinite depth DFS can get lost going down an endless branch and never find the goal even if it exists. This incompleteness is a serious limitation.

Title: BFS is complete and optimal for unit costs
explanation: When all edge costs are equal BFS is both complete and optimal. It finds the shallowest goal node which corresponds to the minimum number of steps.

Title: DFS time complexity misleading
explanation: DFS has the same worst case time complexity O(b^m) as BFS but in practice it can find a solution much faster if the goal happens to be down the first branch explored.

Title: BFS level by level expansion
explanation: Watching BFS expand a tree level by level is very visual and intuitive. Every node at depth d is visited before any node at depth d+1. Clean and systematic.

Title: DFS backtracking mechanism
explanation: The backtracking in DFS is elegant. When a dead end is reached the algorithm simply pops the stack and tries the next sibling branch. Simple recursive implementation.

Title: BFS queue vs DFS stack
explanation: The core data structure difference says it all. BFS uses a queue for FIFO order exploring neighbors first. DFS uses a stack for LIFO order going deep first.

Title: DFS finds non-optimal solutions
explanation: DFS will return the first solution it finds which may not be the shortest or cheapest path. For applications where solution quality matters DFS alone is not sufficient.

Title: Iterative deepening bridges BFS and DFS
explanation: Iterative deepening DFS runs DFS with increasing depth limits. It gets the memory efficiency of DFS and the optimality and completeness of BFS. Best of both worlds.

Title: BFS explores too many useless nodes
explanation: In problems where the goal is deep BFS wastes enormous time exploring all shallow nodes first. Informed search methods like A* are far superior in these cases.

Title: DFS good for maze solving
explanation: DFS is a natural fit for maze solving where you just follow one path until you hit a wall then backtrack. The recursive implementation maps directly to how humans solve mazes.

Title: BFS used in social network analysis
explanation: BFS is widely used in social networks to find the shortest connection between two people. The six degrees of separation problem is essentially a BFS problem.

Title: DFS used in topological sorting
explanation: One of the classic applications of DFS is topological sorting of a directed acyclic graph. The finish times in DFS give a valid topological order.

Title: BFS space complexity kills scalability
explanation: The O(b^d) space complexity of BFS where b is branching factor and d is solution depth means it becomes impractical very quickly for real-world large graphs.

Title: DFS stack overflow risk
explanation: Recursive DFS on very deep graphs can cause stack overflow errors. An iterative implementation using an explicit stack avoids this issue in production code.

Title: Both algorithms need visited tracking
explanation: Both BFS and DFS need a visited or closed set to avoid revisiting nodes in graphs with cycles. Without it both algorithms can loop forever.

Title: BFS optimal only with uniform costs
explanation: An important caveat is that BFS is only optimal when all step costs are identical. As soon as edge weights differ UCS or A* must be used instead.

Title: DFS memory advantage is significant
explanation: DFS only needs to store the current path from root to the current node. This O(b*m) space is dramatically better than BFS when dealing with large deep graphs.

Title: BFS finds all nodes at distance k first
explanation: A useful property of BFS is that it discovers all nodes reachable in exactly k steps before any node at k+1 steps. This makes it ideal for shortest path in unweighted graphs.

Title: DFS can be randomized
explanation: Randomized DFS where child nodes are explored in random order is a common technique for generating random mazes and puzzles. Simple modification with interesting results.

Title: Comparing DFS and BFS on the same graph
explanation: Running both DFS and BFS on the same graph exercise was eye-opening. BFS found the shortest path with 3 edges while DFS found a path with 7 edges. Optimal vs fast tradeoff.

Title: BFS for peer-to-peer networks
explanation: BFS is used in peer-to-peer network protocols to discover nearby peers. Each level of BFS corresponds to one network hop away from the source node.

Title: DFS for detecting cycles
explanation: DFS is the standard algorithm for detecting cycles in a directed graph using back edges. If DFS finds an edge to an ancestor node a cycle exists.

Title: BFS and DFS both uninformed
explanation: Both BFS and DFS are uninformed search algorithms. They have no knowledge about the goal location. Adding heuristics transforms them into much more powerful informed methods.

Title: DFS completeness on finite graphs
explanation: On finite graphs without cycles and with a visited set DFS is actually complete. It will eventually explore every reachable node and find the goal if it exists.

Title: BFS shortest path in web crawling
explanation: Search engines use BFS-like crawling strategies starting from seed URLs and expanding outward layer by layer to discover and index web pages efficiently.

Title: DFS vs BFS depends on the problem
explanation: Choosing between DFS and BFS should depend on the problem structure. If the solution is likely shallow use BFS. If memory is scarce or the graph is deep use DFS.
